reason: The item cleanly tests admissible inference boundaries around (i) rupture-threshold logic, (ii) distributed radical-quenching action, and (iii) separation of endothermic wall effects vs post-rupture chemistry, with correct choices tightly grounded in the stated temperature regime and mechanism descriptions. Decoys are largely plausible and fail for single dominant reasons (mechanism substitution, overclaiming, and unjustified equivalence), creating meaningful expert discrimination. Minor weakness is slight stylistic/quantitative cueing (option D’s conspicuous numbers and option E’s absolute “eliminates”), which can enable partial test-taking heuristics rather than purely mechanism-based reasoning.

The prompt cleanly targets a single expert judgment: recognizing that reduced ignition propensity at lower SOC does not bound overall personnel hazard because toxic off-gassing remains an independent pathway. However, as a SHORT_ANSWER item it has no decoy options, making decoy-quality and some discriminative-strength criteria largely inapplicable under the provided rubric and depressing D2/overall tiering. To improve benchmark diagnostic value, convert to multiple-choice with single-fault decoys (e.g., SOC eliminates TR, gas scales to negligible at 30%, or “no protection needed if no flame”) or add stricter scoring anchors that separate partial from complete hazard-pathway identification.
